name: Alabaster Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Alabaster. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for Alabaster surface cleaning without thermal damage

wavelength.description: Near-IR wavelength providing good absorption in surface contaminants while minimizing bulk material damage

spotSize.description: Beam spot diameter providing sufficient energy density for cleaning with good resolution

repetitionRate.description: Optimal repetition rate for continuous cleaning coverage while avoiding thermal accumulation

fluenceThreshold.description: Energy density threshold for effective contaminant removal without damaging Alabaster substrate

pulseWidth.description: Short pulse duration for efficient contaminant removal with minimal heat diffusion

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for efficient area coverage while maintaining cleaning effectiveness

passCount.description: Recommended number of passes for complete contaminant removal

overlapRatio.description: Optimal beam overlap percentage for uniform cleaning coverage

power.description: Typical range for delicate stone cleaning like alabaster to avoid thermal damage; starting at 50 W for controlled ablation. Max safe ~150 W based on gypsum's low melting point (~150°C). Source: Manufacturer specs from IPG Photonics and academic studies on stone conservation (e.g., J. Cult. Herit. 2018).

scan_speed.description: Slower speeds for heavy contamination on alabaster to ensure removal without substrate heating; faster for light dirt. Recommended 1500 mm/s for balance. Source: Industry standards from Laser Cleaning Europe guidelines and equipment manuals (e.g., CleanLaser systems).

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime preferred for alabaster to minimize heat-affected zone (HAZ); picosecond (ps) or femtosecond for ultra-precision but less common industrially. 20 ns optimal for 1064 nm on low-conductivity materials like gypsum. Source: Laser physics research in Opt. Lasers Eng. (2015) on stone ablation.

repetition_rate.description: Lower rates reduce heat accumulation in thermally sensitive alabaster; higher rates speed up cleaning but risk damage. 30 kHz recommended for effective contamination removal. Trade-off: high freq increases efficiency but elevates surface temp. Source: Academic literature, Appl. Surf. Sci. (2020) on pulsed laser cleaning of minerals.

energy_density.description: Fluence below alabaster's damage threshold (~2 J/cm² for gypsum) for safe cleaning; lower for light contamination. 0.8 J/cm² recommended to ablate contaminants without melting. Conservative due to alabaster's poor thermal conductivity. Source: Ablation threshold studies in J. Appl. Phys. (2017) on calcium sulfate materials.

spot_size.description: Smaller spots for fine detail on alabaster sculptures; larger for broad industrial surfaces. 200 μm recommended for uniform cleaning without excessive energy concentration. Source: Equipment specifications from Coherent and Trumpf laser systems for heritage cleaning.

pass_count.description: Multi-pass for complete removal of heavy contamination on porous alabaster; single pass for light surface prep. 2 passes recommended to minimize total exposure time and heat buildup. Source: Industry practice in conservation case studies (e.g., ICLEA project reports on stone artifacts).

overlap_ratio.description: Higher overlap ensures uniform coverage on uneven alabaster surfaces; 60% balances efficiency and avoids striping. Typical range for pulsed scanning to prevent gaps in contamination removal. Source: Process optimization studies in Lasers Manuf. Mater. Process. (2019).
